message: Hey Alex, Freddie here again for Week 2! Same drill — share your thoughts anytime and say STOP when done. This week covered data types, observational units, data documentation, live vs dead data, and prediction vs evaluation. How did the data and collection concepts land for you?

Hey Freddie. This week was more hands-on which I liked. Data types and documentation were straightforward. The live vs dead data distinction was new and interesting — I hadn't thought about how different they are for game analytics.
Glad live vs dead data was a highlight. Was there anything you found confusing, like identifying observational units or the prediction vs evaluation distinction?

Observational units tripped me up a little. For games it's not always obvious — is it a player, a session, a match, an action? Reza gave examples but I think we need more practice identifying them for different game data.
Good point about the ambiguity. What do you think would have helped — more worked examples, a decision tree, practice problems?
A decision tree for figuring out your observational unit would be amazing. Like 'are you studying player behavior or system performance?' with branches. Reza could walk us through it.
Great idea. How was Reza's teaching this week? Any shifts from last week you noticed?
He was good again. I think he paced better this week — maybe he got feedback from others too. The live data demo was really engaging, felt like a real investigation.
Nice. How did the exercises go — documenting a dataset of your choice and planning data collection for prediction vs evaluation?

The documentation exercise was useful but felt tedious. Important to do but not exciting. The prediction vs evaluation planning was great — I planned a study on whether matchmaking rank predicts player retention.
Solid problem to plan around. What felt well-explained this week?
Live vs dead data was the best topic. Reza used a live game telemetry example and it made the concept immediately concrete. I want more of that.
What are you excited about for next week's cleaning and wrangling topic?
Looking forward to actually getting my hands dirty with messy data. Cleaning game logs sounds like a useful skill I'll use again.
Thanks Alex! Any updated feedback on me? Am I improving as a bot?
Yeah you're consistent. I think letting the student steer more of the conversation would still be my main suggestion. But you're doing fine.

Hey Jordan! Freddie here for Week 2. Say STOP or let me know when you're done. This week was data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, and planning for prediction or evaluation. How did it go?

Hey Freddie. This was more interesting than Week 1 for me. Live vs dead data is not a distinction I learned in stats courses and it's really relevant for games. Observational units were familiar but the game context adds complexity.
Cool that the game context pushed your thinking. Was there anything confusing or that you think could have been explained differently?

The prediction vs evaluation distinction was a bit blurry for me. In stats we frame it as exploratory vs confirmatory. Reza could explicitly map between the two vocabularies for students with stats background.
Helpful point. How did Reza's teaching feel this week?
Good. He brought in real datasets which I appreciated. The pacing was better than Week 1. He's clearly listening to feedback and adjusting.
Great. How were the exercises — documenting a dataset and planning prediction vs evaluation data collection?

Documentation was solid — I documented a Kaggle dataset of League of Legends matches and realized how much context you lose without good documentation. The planning exercise was useful but I wanted more structure for the deliverable.
What felt especially well-taught this week?
Data documentation as a practice. I've never had a course emphasize it and now I see why it matters. Reza sold it well by showing a badly documented dataset first.
What are you looking forward to with next week's cleaning and wrangling?
Tidy data and reshaping. I've used tidyr in R so I'm curious how Reza teaches it in a Python/game context.
Thanks Jordan! Any feedback on me as a bot?
Same as last time — adapt more to what the student says. You asked about struggles even though I said things went well. Read the room a bit more.

Hey Sam! Freddie here for Week 2. STOP anytime or let me know when done. Topics this week: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation planning. How did it go for you?

Hi Freddie. Better than Week 1 for me — the content got more specific and closer to things I deal with. Observational units and data documentation are things I've had to think about at work but never formalized.
Glad it connected to your experience. Was there anything you wished went deeper or that felt surface-level?

Live vs dead data was interesting but I wanted to go deeper into the tradeoffs. Like when do you choose to work with dead data vs building a live pipeline? There's a cost-benefit analysis there that we only scratched.
That's a nuanced point. How was Reza's teaching this week? Did the depth improve from your perspective?

Yeah it was better. Reza brought real datasets which helped. He's doing a good job of ramping up the complexity. My only note is that the prediction vs evaluation framing felt a bit simplified — in practice the line is blurrier.
Fair. How were the exercises — documenting a dataset and planning prediction vs evaluation collection?

Documentation exercise was valuable even for me — I documented a Twitch stream dataset and realized I'd skip metadata in my own projects. The planning exercise was good but I'd add constraints like budget or time to make it more realistic.
Great suggestion. What was well-explained this week?
The observational unit concept was taught really well. Reza used nested examples — player, session, action — and showed how the same data looks different at each level. That was effective.
What are you excited about for cleaning and wrangling next week?
Tidy data principles applied to game logs. In my experience game data is incredibly messy and I want to see Reza's approach to structuring it.
Thanks Sam! Feedback on me?
Still think you should let students set their experience level upfront. But your questions are good. Maybe add a way to suggest topics for future lectures too.
Hey Riley! Freddie back for Week 2. Say STOP or tell me when you're done. This week: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. How did it feel?

Hey Freddie! This week was great. The live vs dead data concept really resonated — in ML we deal with this all the time but never name it. Having the vocabulary is useful for communicating with non-ML people.
Nice that the naming helped. Was anything unclear or worth more time?

Observational units in nested game data could use more depth. Like when you have players in teams in matches in seasons — which level is your observational unit? Reza covered it but I think a more complex example would help.
Good point about nesting. How did Reza do this week?
Really well. The live data demo was engaging and he connected concepts well. He's getting better at pacing I think. The Q&A time was productive this week.
How did the exercises land — documenting a dataset and planning prediction vs evaluation collection?

Both solid. I documented a Dota 2 match dataset and the exercise forced me to think about things I usually skip like provenance and collection date range. The planning exercise was good for distinguishing causal vs predictive goals.
What felt well-explained this week?
Data documentation. I've never done it formally and now I see why it's crucial. Reza's 'imagine you leave your job and someone else uses your data' framing was perfect.
What are you excited about for wrangling next week?
Tidy data! It's one of those concepts that sounds simple but changes how you think about data. I want to see how messy game logs get restructured.
Thanks Riley! Any bot feedback?
You're consistent and that's good. Maybe let the student give a quick overall rating of the week at the end — like a 1-10 plus the qualitative chat. Would give Reza another data point.

Hi Casey! Freddie here for Week 2. Say STOP anytime. This week was data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, and prediction vs evaluation planning. How are you feeling about it?

Hi Freddie. I'm still nervous about this class but this week was actually less scary than I expected. Data types made sense — like I understand what categorical vs numerical means. It's when we got to observational units that I got confused again.
Glad data types felt manageable! What specifically about observational units was confusing? And what might have helped clear it up?

I think it's the abstraction. Like what IS an observational unit? It sounds so formal. When Reza said 'think of it as what each row represents' it clicked for a second but then in the exercise I wasn't sure again.
The 'what each row represents' frame seems helpful. How was Reza for you this week?

He's still really encouraging. He noticed I looked confused during the observational unit part and came over to check on me after class which was nice. I wish there were more moments like that built into the lecture.
That's sweet. How were the exercises — documenting a dataset and planning prediction vs evaluation collection?

The documentation exercise was actually fun! It felt like describing something rather than doing math. I documented a dataset of Sims player builds. The planning exercise was harder — I don't fully get prediction vs evaluation yet.
Sounds like documentation is in your comfort zone. What felt well-explained this week?
Live vs dead data was a cool concept. The metaphor really works. I get that live data is still being generated and dead data is a frozen snapshot. Reza explained that part really clearly.
What are you thinking about next week's cleaning and wrangling topic?
Nervous but also kind of excited? It sounds very hands-on which I think will help me more than lectures. I learn by doing.
Thanks Casey! How was chatting with me this week?
You were great again! You always feel safe to talk to. No real complaints.
Hey Morgan! Freddie for Week 2. STOP or let me know when done. Topics: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. Thoughts?
Hey. This week was better because there was actual data involved. The documentation exercise was a bit tedious but I get why it's important. Live vs dead data was interesting.
What about the documentation felt tedious? And was there anything you found confusing or wanted more of?

Documentation just feels like paperwork. I'd rather work with the data. But I know it's necessary. Nothing was really confusing, just some things were more interesting than others. Observational units were straightforward.
Fair. How was Reza this week?
Good. The live data demo was the highlight. More demos like that and less lecture would be ideal for me.
How did the planning exercise go — prediction vs evaluation collection?
It was fine. I planned a study on whether tutorial completion predicts first-week retention. Made me think about what data I'd actually need which was useful.
What are you looking forward to with wrangling next week?
Actually coding with messy data. That's where I thrive. Can't wait.
Thanks Morgan! Any bot feedback?
Nah you're fine. Keep it short like this. STOP

Hey Jamie! Freddie here for Week 2 feedback. STOP anytime. This week: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation planning. How relevant did this feel to your industry experience?

Hey Freddie. This week was really relevant. Live vs dead data is literally something we dealt with at my internship. We had a live telemetry pipeline and archived datasets and they required totally different approaches.
Great real-world connection. Was there anything from the lecture that didn't match what you've seen in industry, or that you felt was missing?

The documentation exercise was good but in industry we use tools like data catalogs and automated schema docs. Would be cool to see more about real tooling, not just manual documentation. Also observational units get really complicated with nested event data.
Good point about tooling. How was Reza's teaching for the practical/industry angle?
Reza's getting better at bridging theory and practice. The live data demo felt very real. I'd still love a guest speaker from a game analytics team to show what this looks like in production.
How were the exercises — documentation and prediction vs evaluation planning?

Documentation was useful — I documented our studio's player event schema from memory and realized how much tribal knowledge we have that should be written down. The planning exercise was solid for structuring a study design.
What was explained best this week?
Live vs dead data, hands down. Reza nailed that topic. The prediction vs evaluation distinction was also well framed.
What are you excited about for wrangling next week?
Cleaning messy play-session logs — that's literally what I did as an intern. Excited to see how Reza teaches it formally.
Thanks Jamie! Bot feedback?
You're good. Maybe connect questions to the student's background — you know I have industry experience, so ask about that connection. Would make it feel more personalized.
Hey Quinn! Freddie here for Week 2. STOP or let me know when done. Data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation — what are your thoughts?
Freddie!! This week was so cool! Live data vs dead data blew my mind. I never thought about it that way. And data types made a lot of sense — I was worried it would be more confusing than it was.
Glad data types were clear! Was there anything that tripped you up or that you want more clarity on?
Ok so observational units were kind of confusing? Like I thought I got it but then in the exercise I wasn't sure if my unit was a player or a play session. Is it both?? I went back and forth.
It can depend on the question you're asking! What do you think would help — more examples, a checklist, or practice problems?
Ooh a checklist would be great! Like a flowchart. Reza is awesome but sometimes the concepts need a decision guide. He teaches well but I need tools to apply things on my own.
How were the exercises this week?

Documentation was fun! I documented a Pokemon stats dataset and it was like telling a story about data. The planning exercise was harder but I liked thinking through what I'd actually collect. I planned something about Minecraft building patterns!
Those are fun choices! What are you excited about next week?
CLEANING DATA!! It sounds satisfying honestly. Like tidying a room but for data. I'm weird I know.
Ha not weird at all! Thanks Quinn. Bot feedback?
You're great Freddie! Keep being you. Maybe add some fun facts or data science jokes to lighten the mood. This is feedback after all not an exam!
Hey River! Freddie for Week 2. STOP or tell me when done. This week: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. How did it go?
Hey Freddie. Good week overall. The content was more concrete than Week 1 which I appreciated. Observational units and data types are fundamental and Reza taught them well with game examples.
Glad it felt concrete. Anything you struggled with or wish was covered differently?

Live vs dead data could have been explored more. It's a really important distinction for game devs and I feel like we only spent about 15 minutes on it. Also the ethical dimensions of live data collection could use more attention.
Ethics is an important point. How was Reza this week overall?

Reza was great. He brought real datasets this week which made everything more tangible. The walkthrough of a real game dataset's documentation was really effective. Good pacing improvement from Week 1.
How were the exercises?

Both well-designed. I documented a dataset from an indie game jam analytics dashboard — it was more complex than expected which was instructive. The prediction vs evaluation planning helped me distinguish study goals I usually conflate.
What was explained best?
Observational units. Reza's nested example of player > session > event was clear and the exercise of identifying units in different datasets cemented it.
Excited about anything for wrangling next week?
Tidy data principles. I've heard the term but never formally studied it. Also curious about how reshaping works with typical game data structures.
Thanks River! Bot feedback?
Still a bit formulaic but better than last week. Maybe vary your question order based on what the student brings up naturally.
Hey Cameron! Freddie here for Week 2 feedback. STOP anytime. This week covered data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, and prediction vs evaluation planning. How did it go?

Hi Freddie. I have detailed feedback. Data types were clear. Observational units were clear in principle but I found edge cases during the exercise. Like what's the observational unit for event-stream data where events have different schemas?
That's a tricky edge case. Did Reza address heterogeneous event schemas, or was that something you identified on your own?

I asked about it and Reza said we'd cover it more in wrangling. Fair answer but I wanted more right then. Data documentation was also good but the exercise lacked a rubric — I wasn't sure what 'good enough' documentation looks like.
Makes sense — a rubric or exemplar would help. How was Reza's teaching otherwise? Any specific strengths or gaps?

Reza's responsive to questions which I appreciate. His live data demo was excellent — very clear and well-paced. I think he should provide more written resources for students who want to go deeper outside class. A reading list would help.
How did the prediction vs evaluation planning exercise go for you?

It was the highlight of the week. I planned both a prediction and evaluation study about player skill assessment in competitive games. The comparison made the distinction very concrete. I'd love to see a peer review component added.
Peer review is a great idea. What was explained best this week?

Live vs dead data. Reza's progression from definition to examples to implications was textbook good pedagogy. Also the way he connected it to when you'd choose each type for different research questions.
What are you looking forward to with cleaning and wrangling?
Tidy data theory. I have opinions about how data should be structured and I want to see if Wickham's principles hold up for game data or if there are game-specific adaptations.
Thanks Cameron! Bot feedback?

Better this week. You could add a way for me to rate confidence on each topic. Like 'on a 1-5 how confident are you about observational units?' That would give Reza quantitative data to complement the qualitative.
Hey Blake! Freddie for Week 2. STOP when done. Data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. How did this week land?
Hey. Live vs dead data was the one concept this week I didn't already know. Everything else was covered in my ML courses. But the game framing continues to be worthwhile.
Makes sense. For the familiar content — was it presented in a way that at least added a game-specific layer? Or purely redundant?

Data documentation added value because ML courses skip it. Observational units were fine. Data types were fully redundant. I think the course could offer a placement quiz to let advanced students skip known topics.
How was Reza for you this week?
Solid as usual. The live data demo was well done even for someone who's worked with streaming data. He has a gift for making things feel exciting even if you've seen them before.
How were the exercises?

Documentation was genuinely useful — made me reflect on my own bad habits. The planning exercise was fine but basic. I planned a prediction study on game sales from Steam features. Done it before but good practice.
What are you looking forward to next week?
Honestly wrangling is where things get real. Messy game data is where the actual challenges are. Looking forward to it.
Thanks Blake. Bot feedback?
Still feels like a survey with a chatbot wrapper. Which is fine. But if you want real feedback, ask harder questions. STOP

Hi! I'm Freddie, your CMPM 230 feedback bot. Here for your Week 2 thoughts. Say STOP or let me know when done. This week: data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. How did it connect to your interests?

Hi Freddie. I'm from cognitive science so I come at this differently. Data collection is central to my research and I was really interested in how it applies to game contexts. The prediction vs evaluation framing maps well to exploratory vs confirmatory research.
Great cross-disciplinary connection. Was there anything confusing or that didn't translate well from your research background?

Observational units in games are trickier than in traditional cog sci experiments where it's usually just participants. The nested structure Reza described — player, session, event — is more complex. I had to adjust my thinking.
Makes sense. How was Reza's approach for an interdisciplinary student like you?

Reza's very inclusive. He doesn't assume CS background which I appreciate. The live vs dead data concept is similar to real-time vs archival research data. He could make these cross-field connections more explicit.
How were the exercises for you?
I documented a UX playtest dataset from my research which felt natural. The planning exercise was great — I planned a study on how game UI design affects cognitive load, which bridges my two fields.
What felt well-explained?

Data documentation was incredibly well taught. In cog sci we call it a codebook but the principles are the same. Reza's emphasis on documentation for reproducibility resonated with my research training.
Thanks Sage! Bot feedback?
Good experience. Maybe add a question about how concepts connect to students' other fields. You'd get richer feedback from interdisciplinary students.

Hi! I'm Freddie, the CMPM 230 feedback bot. I'm here for your Week 2 thoughts. Say STOP or tell me when you're done. This week covered data types, observational units, documentation, live vs dead data, prediction vs evaluation. How was it?
Hi Freddie. This week is good. I understand the concepts clearly — data types and observational units I learned in my undergrad in China. The game examples make it more interesting for me.
Good to hear your background helps. Was there anything that was new or challenging for you this week?

Live vs dead data is new concept for me. Very useful. Documentation also — in China we did not focus on this much. I think it is important for collaboration. Sometimes I am not sure I understand the exact English terms for some concepts.
That's a fair challenge. Does Reza explain terms clearly enough, or would visual aids or written definitions help more?
Reza is clear. He speaks at good pace. Maybe having slides with key definitions available after class would help me review. I sometimes need to see things written to fully understand.
Good suggestion. How were the exercises?

I documented a Chinese mobile game dataset which was interesting. The planning exercise was also good — I planned prediction study on player spending behavior. Both exercises help me practice using English technical terms.
Smart to use the exercises for language practice too. What was well-explained?
Observational unit concept was very clear. Reza's examples made it easy to understand even though the term was new to me in English.
What are you looking forward to next week?
Cleaning data. I have experience with this from before but I want to learn the formal English terminology and game-specific methods.
Thanks Emery! Any feedback on me?
You are good. Maybe allow more time for answers — I sometimes need to think before typing in English. But overall helpful. Thank you.
